preFix.title: Horseradish cream
preFix.subtitle: Freshly grated, folded into whipped cream
preFix.excerpt: Freshly grated horseradish folded into lightly whipped double cream with lemon and mustard. Sharp, sinus-clearing, and far superior to the jarred version for roast beef.
preFix.sourceNotes:
Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. Horseradish sauce for roast beef.

Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845). Horseradish cream.

content.text: Fresh horseradish loses its heat within an hour of grating. If you want the full effect, make this sauce close to serving. The whipped cream base softens the impact of the horseradish and allows you to add significantly more than you would use in a vinegar-based sauce without the heat being overwhelming.
Grate fresh horseradish outside or near an open window. The fumes are intense and affect the eyes and nose immediately. Use a fine grater and grate directly onto a plate to contain the vapour.

items.name: Fresh horseradish
items.prepNote: finely grated (or 3 tbsp from a jar, well-drained)

content.text: Whip the cold cream in a chilled bowl until it just holds soft peaks. Do not overwhip to stiff peaks: a soft texture is better for a sauce.
Mix the grated horseradish, mustard, and lemon juice together in a small bowl.
this into the whipped cream gently, keeping the texture light. Season with a pinch of salt.
Taste and adjust: add more horseradish if you want more heat, more lemon for sharpness. Serve within 2 hours.

items.fix: Always use cold cream in a cold bowl. Stop whipping at soft peaks. If it looks grainy, stir in a tablespoon of cold cream to bring it back.
items.cause: Overwhipped, or the cream was warm.
items.symptom: The cream has gone grainy and curdled-looking

items.fix: Fresh horseradish must be grated to order. A root bought and grated days before will have lost most of its heat.
items.cause: The horseradish root was old or stored too long after grating.
items.symptom: The horseradish heat is gone even though it was just made

items.fix: Add more whipped cream to balance. The lemon is background brightness, not the main flavour.
items.cause: Too much lemon juice relative to the cream.
items.symptom: The sauce tastes too sharp from the lemon

items.fix: If using jarred, drain it well on kitchen paper before folding in. Squeeze out excess liquid gently.
items.cause: Jarred horseradish contains vinegar and has a high water content.
items.symptom: The jarred horseradish is making the cream watery

content.text: For a lighter version, use creme fraiche instead of whipped cream. A tablespoon of grated fresh ginger alongside the horseradish adds a different heat and works particularly well with cold roast beef.

Horseradish cream is the classic companion to roast beef in Britain. Victorian cookery writer Mrs Beeton described a vinegar-based version. Double cream became widely available in the twentieth century, and the cream version took over. The jarred sauce in a shop is a fair substitute but gives a much milder result.
